title: Accelerate LLM post training with W&B Serverless SFT
transcript: Hi, I'm Russ from Weights & Biases, and today I'll be discussing how W&B training serverless SFT, powered by CoreWeave, helps AI engineers use curated data sets for model distillation, customize model output format and style, and warm models up for RL training so they start from a stronger baseline. As the barrier to entry for building AI application systems diminished over the past several months, both the number of AI agents and the number of developers building AI agents have grown exponentially. We're now in a world where more often than not, agents are building other agents during interactive vibe coding sessions or much longer set-it-and-forget-it runs. But even as the proliferation of agents has exploded, the exact same challenge remains. It's easier than ever to build demos or prototypes, but hard to productionize. Even after debugging and ironing out coding issues, agent performance must be optimized along multiple dimensions, including accuracy, latency, cost, and safety. There are a number of ways to optimize agents, including prompt engineering, retrieval augmented generation or RAG, and LLM slopping. More resource-intensive post-training techniques include reinforcement learning and supervised fine-tuning. Pre-trained large language models are strong next token predictors, but depending on what purpose they're serving, a customer support agent, a research agent, a coding agent, they usually benefit from fine-tuning to handle specific tasks like question answering, reasoning, and other agentic actions. Supervised fine-tuning, or SFT, is one of the most efficient ways to teach LLMs how to perform specific tasks. And combining SFT with reinforcement learning, or RL, has become a trusted approach for producing reliable, production-ready agents. In fact, it's become common for teams to repeat the cycle between SFT and RL, refining performance until the agent meets deployment standards. But executing this loop between SFT and RL is easier said than done. The problem is that alternating between SFT and RL means potentially moving model checkpoints and artifacts back and forth between different systems, or storing and managing model weights between runs depending on evaluation and decision-making schedules. These challenges impede rapid iteration and can delay optimization progress and ultimately time to market. W&B training serverless SFT removes the obstacles associated with post-training agents, allowing AI engineers to switch quickly between SFT and RL as often as needed without shuttling model artifacts across different systems. With W&B training, engineers receive instant, seamless access to CoreWeave GPU capacity. This means no infrastructure headaches as provisioning, scaling, and optimizing are all handled for you automatically. To get started, you just call the open-source agent reinforcement trainer, or ART, API, specifying your data set and a base model. During and after the SFT run, resulting LoRA adapters are saved directly to W&B artifacts. Serve up these model weights using W&B inference, collect traces, and then run serverless RL to improve reliability on agentic tasks. Repeat the cycle as many times as needed to reach the performance levels required to move your agent into production. To demonstrate serverless SFT, we're going to use a simple coding agent that we've developed and post-trained using W&B training. Our agent can handle application improvement requests and general coding questions. Inputs are delivered to the planner agent, which has a couple of tools at its disposal for considering and acting upon the request. And then the planner agent output, often consisting of code changes or recommendations, is sent to a review agent for a quality assessment and approval or rejection before sending a response back to the user. We've run through some initial evaluations using proprietary and open-source base models. Let's take a look at the results using weave before we jump into some post-training. Weave helps developers evaluate, monitor, and iterate continuously to deliver AI agents with confidence. I've run a bunch of evaluations on our coding agent, specifically examining performance across multiple dimensions using different planner agent LLMs. In this case, all of them base models with no fine-tuning. Right off the bat, our Qwen 3 model looks decent, but still didn't score quite as high in terms of accuracy as our GPT models. However, as to be expected with our open-source model here, latency and cost look great. Let's jump over to W&B training to see how we can improve our agent model, starting with serverless SFT. And then also take a look at how easy it is to transition between serverless SFT and serverless RL to optimize model performance and reliability. To get started quickly with serverless SFT, just check out the W&B training docs at docs.wandb.ai/training. Here you'll find a product overview and a link that'll take you directly to the serverless SFT page with detailed examples and some sample code for common SFT use cases, including distillation, where a smaller model learns from the outputs of a larger teacher model, and RL warm-up, where you use SFT to give the model a head start, then switch to RL for further improvement. Coming back to our coding agent, our goal with serverless SFT is to teach our LLM a specific output style or format to use when responding to developer queries. Our desire for consistency and brevity in the output is twofold. One, we expect our users to expect and appreciate concise, to-the-point responses. And two, less tokens, lower cost. For our training data set, we're using the Code Alpaca 20k data set. For fine-tuning an LLM, this data set can help the model become better at following code-related instructions, recognizing the difference between a code question and an application coding request, and producing responses in a more useful, developer-oriented format. And for our base model, we've started off with Qwen 34B, served up using W&B inference powered by CoreWeave. Of course, W&B inference offers a wide selection of open-source AI models, so as we progress, we'll plan to give a few others a try as well. You can see all of our training runs here on the left, and our results here on the right. Again, we're looking for that sweet spot combination of accuracy plus minimal tokens, and we don't mind low latency, either. In addition to the standard training and validation metrics that we're collecting, we're also running weave evaluations on our agent using the fine-tuned LoRA weights after every epoch. The ability to include weave panels directly in our W&B workspace and build charts using our evaluation results gives us all of the data points we need to track the progress of our serverless SFT run and decide on our next steps. These fine-tuned models are certainly looking promising. Are they ready for some final testing and then deployment? Or should we kick off another run adjusting configuration parameters and maybe trying out a different base model? Another option is to pursue our serverless SFT serverless RL training loop to see whether that might further improve performance. Because we've been running evaluations on models after every epoch, we know at which steps they've been demonstrating the best performance. Using W&B training, we can fork and start our serverless RL runs from the optimal serverless SFT checkpoints. Serverless SFT has gotten us the output style and formatting that we want. And now we can use serverless RL to achieve our ideal agent behavior providing the best possible experience for our end user by generating accurate code and concise easy-to-understand Just as before in our serverless SFT workspace, I'm also consistently running weave evaluations during my RL runs so I can continually track model progress along all of my important performance metrics. And once we've got Laura weights that are good production candidates, we can serve them up for final testing and production deployment using W&B inference serverless Laura inference. This run here seems to produce the best results. To get instructions for accessing the Laura's, I simply click on the run name and And click the artifacts link up top. And then click on the final set of weights here. Scroll down to the inference access section. And I've got sample Python and curl code syntax to get me started. I can also click the try in playground button and test the model out in the weave playground for a quick interactive test. Just ask a coding question right here. Great to get an initial sanity check. But of course, we may want to run this one through a final round of thorough rigorous e-vals before we make it available to our end users. Combining W&B training and W&B inference offers the opportunity to achieve state-of-the-art performance with lower latency and that a fraction of the cost. The Weights & Biases AI developer platform eliminates the barriers associated with infrastructure requirements and post-training expertise, making it easier to build production-ready models. Thanks for your time. And if you haven't already, head on over to wandb.ai, sign up for a free account, and give the Weights & Biases AI developer platform a try today.
